SourceString: A pigment to dye leather and fabric. Never runs out once obtained.

Template for books with multiple volumes, e.g. "Scales of Steel and Terror, Vol. 2"
Listing that there's more sword parts contained in a book. 0: Count
Listing a sword part contained in a book. 0: Category, 1: Part Name
This alludes both to symmetrical shapes created with turning (e.g. woodturning), and also to scent stopper pommels, i.e. pommels that look like perfume caps.
Talindh is a proper name, the kingdom in which Eren Keep is located.

SourceString: A History of the World in Seven Swords
An essential metal for swordmaking, necessary for forging the blade itself.
A common material for swordmaking, used in hilts. In shorter supply than it once was.
A useful material for swordmaking, wrapped around grips for comfort and control.

A precious metal for swordmaking, used for purely decorative purposes. Once associated with Ouzoma, the Voice of the Sun and Fate, who is said to have created it to entice mortals with dreams of a better future.
A precious metal for swordmaking, used for purely decorative purposes. Once associated with Myrmec, the Voice of the Moon and Wisdom, and said to ward against shape-changers.
A grim reminder of your past. Melt them down and start anew.
Rusty implements past their prime. Only good for salvage.
Shattered spokes, a cracked rim. Will never carry another cart, but perhaps you can turn it to some new purpose.
A lonely goat-leather boot, missing its brother.
Is that blood or rust speckling the edges of the cuffs?
The leather’s torn, the wood cracked. When it comes to drums, you can’t beat this one.
Better hide this well. The gleam of gold attracts jealousy like flies.
A trinket of modest worth. Someone once treasured this bauble.

A sophisticated bronze brooch, passed down through your family line for untold generations. It’s the only thing you have from before the Sundering. You’ve never been able to translate the inscription around the edge.
A bag of well-worn leather, clasped tight with drawstrings. Can be opened to retrieve whatever’s inside.
A box engraved with beautifully intricate carvings. Can be opened to retrieve whatever’s inside.

Liquid amber bliss in a cloudy glass bottle. The last surviving example of this particular vintage. You’ve treasured it for so long, you’re not sure there’ll ever be an occasion special enough to drink it.
A roughly-hewn statuette depicting a blacksmith at the forge. Aw, look at his widdle hammer.
Sold as a cure for insomnia, this puts the imbiber into a long and dreamless sleep.
It drinks the light and eats the mind. When you look at it too closely, you find that an hour’s passed and you have a headache.
A dagger salvaged from the smouldering ruins of a paper mill. Though scorched black, it’s the only clue as to the identity of the arsonist.
Now it’s clean, it’s evident that this dagger is of fine craftsmanship.
The common blacksmith’s bread and butter, horseshoes were once a symbol of Skell and were alleged to bring good luck.
A drinking vessel and, if you’re drunk enough, percussive instrument.
This implies that somewhere there’s a ruined head.
An unassuming leather collar with a simple brass ring. Light enough that it won’t slow a good dog down.
Thick padded leather, built to protect the throat from blade and claw.
